title: ASA uses AI to spot alcohol ad breaches
text:
ASA uses AI to spot alcohol ad breaches in ‘first-of-its kind’ trial
The regulator’s trial used artificial intelligence to scan nearly 6,000 alcohol-related ads, finding widespread labelling issues among alcohol-free products.
By
Grace Gollasch
9 Oct 2025
12:01 am
X
Facebook
LinkedIn
N
early half (48%) of ads for alcohol-free products breach advertising rules, according to
a
“world-leading” trial using
artificial intelligence conducted by the
Advertising Standards Authority (ASA).
The AI analysis found most breaches related to unclear or missing alcohol by volume (ABV) labelling – a key requirement for consumers who avoid alcohol for health, religious or personal reasons.
Despite this, the ASA reported 96% of alcohol-related ads overall appeared to comply with advertising rules, with only 1% to 3% showing likely violations. A further 1% of ads required additional review.
N
early half (48%) of ads for alcohol-free products breach advertising rules, according to
a
“world-leading” trial using
artificial intelligence conducted by the
Advertising Standards Authority (ASA).
The AI analysis found most breaches related to unclear or missing alcohol by volume (ABV) labelling – a key requirement for consumers who avoid alcohol for health, religious or personal reasons.
Despite this, the ASA reported 96% of alcohol-related ads overall appeared to comply with advertising rules, with only 1% to 3% showing likely violations. A further 1% of ads required additional review.
Common issues among those breaking the rules included misleading or unauthorised health or nutritional claims, ads promoting irresponsible drinking and content likely to have particular appeal to children. Breaches were identified across both large hospitality chains and smaller independent advertisers.
The ASA’s director of data science, Adam Davison, says the report proves AI can be used to help experts “scan large volumes of ads and check them against lots of complex rules more quickly”.
“It highlights that the alcohol sector is generally sticking to the rules, which is great news. We’ve also spotted places where we think we can do more, and deliver better regulation thanks to our rapid adoption of AI,” he says.
“We believe AI has the potential to make our online ad regulation much faster and more effective.”
ASA bans Zoe ad over ‘misleading’ ultra-processed food claims
Using its AI-based Active Ad Monitoring System, the ASA analysed nearly 6,000 paid-for UK ads in early 2025, covering alcoholic drinks, alcohol-free alternatives, promotions from venues and events, and other alcohol-related contexts across search, display and social media.
Each ad, including its text and imagery, was assessed by large language models (LLMs) – advanced AI tools which can interpret and apply ASA rules.
Ads flagged by the LLMs as being at risk of breaking the rules were then reviewed by human experts to determine whether the content was a likely breach. This process combined the scale and speed of AI with expert human judgement to ensure accuracy.
The ASA has used AI to monitor online advertising since 2023, but this marks the first time it has deployed the technology to assess such a large number of ads against an entire section of the advertising rules.
